title: Threading and dressing a four-shaft floor loom
subtitle: The full sequence from winding the warp to beaming, threading, sleying, and tying on
excerpt: Threading a four-shaft floor loom is a multi-stage process that takes several hours for a first project warp. Each stage must be completed correctly before the next: a threading error discovered at weaving time means un-threading and re-threading from that point. The reward is a loom capable of producing twill, overshot, and other shaft-based structures not possible on a rigid heddle.
sourceNotes: Loom dressing sequence follows the standard described in Hooper, Hand-Loom Weaving (1910), Internet Archive. The four-shaft sequence is consistent with the Black tradition referenced in contemporary weaving instruction.

glossaryTerms.definition: The back beam on the loom that holds the wound warp threads under tension. The weaver advances the warp by releasing and re-tensioning the warp beam as weaving progresses.
A thin metal or string loop through which one warp thread passes. Heddles hang on shafts; lifting a shaft raises all the warp threads threaded through its heddles, forming a shed.
A comb-like device through which the warp threads are sleyed (threaded). The reed determines the sett (threads per centimetre) and is used as a beater to push each weft pick against the previous one.
The process of threading the warp ends through the dents of the reed, one or two ends per dent as the sett requires.

aliases: how to dress a floor loom

content.text: The five stages of dressing a four-shaft loom are: (1) wind the warp; (2) load the warp onto the loom and spread it in the raddle; (3) beam the warp onto the
warp beam
; (4) thread the
heddles
according to the threading draft; (5) sley the
reed
and tie onto the cloth beam.
Sleying
errors and threading errors are the two most common sources of weaving problems; catching them before the weaving begins saves time.
What you need

items.name: Four-shaft floor loom with raddle, reed, and two shuttles
Warp yarn (cotton or wool, 8-12 EPI for a first project)
Lease sticks (2, usually supplied with loom)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Wind the warp on the warping board

Calculate warp length (finished project length + 50 cm for loom waste) and number of ends (sett in EPI multiplied by finished width in inches). Wind the warp at an even tension on the warping board, creating a figure-8 cross at the starting peg. The cross preserves thread order for threading.
Step 2: Transfer to the loom and spread in the raddle

Insert the lease sticks through the cross before removing the warp from the warping board. Tie the chained warp to the back beam apron rod. Spread the warp across the raddle at the correct width (one dent of the raddle per 1 cm of warp width, approximately). The raddle keeps the warp spread evenly as it is beamed.
Step 3: Beam the warp

Have a second person hold the warp ends under tension while you wind the warp beam. Insert separator sticks or paper between each layer to prevent threads from digging into previous layers. Beam firmly and evenly. Remove the raddle once beaming is complete.
Step 4: Thread the heddles

Sit at the front of the loom, facing the heddles. Work from the threading draft, threading each warp end through its assigned heddle in the correct shaft. A straight draw (1,2,3,4,1,2,3,4...) is the standard beginning threading for four-shaft twill. Check every 10 ends against the draft to catch errors early.
Step 5: Sley the reed and tie on

Sley the warp through the reed according to the sett (e.g. 10 EPI into a 10-dent reed, one thread per dent). Work from the centre outward to keep the warp centred. Tie the warp groups onto the front apron rod with slip knots, checking tension across the full width before weaving the header.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two common threading errors and how to catch them before weaving.

items.symptom: A pair of warp ends is crossed or twisted in the same heddle
items.cause: Two threads were passed through one heddle together rather than singly, or the cross was lost during threading.
items.fix: Check each heddle eye holds exactly one warp end. Cross the lease sticks to find the order; re-thread any errors before sleying.

items.symptom: Shed does not open cleanly — threads from multiple shafts rise and drop together
items.cause: Threading error: two threads assigned to different shafts are threaded through the same shaft's heddles.
items.fix: Check the threading draft against the actual heddle sequence in groups of 10. Correct any threading errors before moving to sleying.
